target.name: Altos Labs
target.founder: Hal Barron, Rick Klausner, Hans Bishop

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Late Stage → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $3.0B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Biotech & Health (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 16 same-sector peers exist in Biotech & Health, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Biotech & Health P/S median is 7.8x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Biotech & Health

data.narrative:
Altos Labs is a late-stage biotech company founded by Hal Barron, Rick Klausner, and Hans Bishop that has raised $3.0B to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 3 public peers in Biotech & Health (RXRX, TEM, ONT.L) and 8 private companies including Tempus AI, Abridge, and Ultima Genomics, though narrow-filter peer matching yielded insufficient depth for direct multiples.

A stage-progression method, applicable to late-stage financings, applies a 1.2x step-up multiplier to the $3.0B raised proxy, implying a valuation of $3.6B. This approach captures typical valuation expansion from cumulative capital deployment at this maturity level. Public comps in the sector trade at a 7.8x median price-to-sales multiple, but this cannot be applied directly without disclosed revenue figures—standard for private companies. A peer-median approach was not feasible given the absence of three or more directly comparable late-stage biotech companies with sufficient data transparency.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $3.1B to $4.1B, with a median implied value of $3.6B. This single-method triangulation reflects meaningful uncertainty: the lack of revenue disclosure prevents revenue-based anchoring, and the sparse comparable set limits cross-validation. Key sensitivities include near-term financing activity, pipeline advancement, and disclosed financials from peer companies that would enable future P/S or EV-based refinement.
